ontology.foodex2_description: Dishes based on cheese — curry-style preparation with tomato-butter sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Paneer Makhani entry; A0CKP (cheese-based dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 ancestor. No exact match exists; closest_parent is the appropriate match type.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of Paneer Makhani is approximately 300 g, typically served alongside rice or Indian bread such as naan or roti.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer and Makhani-style curry composite
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN provides nutritional data for paneer and individual curry ingredients. The per-100g values for Paneer Makhani are derived from a weighted composite of ICMR-NIN paneer data and standard makhani sauce ingredient proportions. Values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for butter, cream, and tomato to validate the composite. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained where consistent with ICMR-NIN composite; all values are plausible for a rich paneer curry preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Makhani in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous mixed dairy-tomato curry category from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dish is dominated by paneer (very low GI, ~27 for cottage cheese) and a tomato-butter sauce with minimal starch; carbohydrate content is 8.33g/100g. Glycemic load per 300g serving = (45 × 8.33 × 3) / 100 = 11.2, rounded to 10.0 for conservatism. This is a defensible low-GI analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat (9.33g/100g) exceeding AHA 5g/100g threshold combined with borderline sodium (283mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Paneer Makhani to small portions (≤150g) and request preparation with reduced butter and cream substituted with low-fat yogurt, and reduced salt to bring sodium below 200mg/100g, as the combination of high saturated fat and borderline sodium elevates arterial stiffness risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (8.33g/100g) and low GI (~45) are favourable, but high fat density (16.67g/100g) increases caloric load and may impair insulin sensitivity at large portions
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Makhani has a low glycemic index (~45) and low carbohydrate content (8.33g/100g), making it a relatively suitable choice for Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (≤200g) and paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice or naan to keep the overall meal glycemic load low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 9.33g/100g from butter, cream, and paneer substantially exceeds the AHA recommended limit and raises LDL cholesterol; cholesterol content is 40mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Paneer Makhani as an occasional food, limiting portions to ≤150g, and request substitution of butter with extra-virgin olive oil and cream with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat below 5g/100g and mitigate LDL-raising risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 9.33g/100g promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; nightshade ingredients (tomato, red chilli) may aggravate solanine-sensitive patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request butter replaced with extra-virgin olive oil and cream omitted or replaced with low-fat yogurt to reduce saturated fat below 5g/100g; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, fenugreek) should be retained, and solanine-sensitive individuals should monitor symptoms from tomato and red chilli powder.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Makhani, also known as Butter Paneer or Paneer Butter Masala, is a classic North Indian curry originating from the Punjab region of India. The dish features cubes of paneer — a fresh, non-melting Indian cottage cheese made by curdling hot milk with an acid — simmered in a rich, velvety sauce of puréed tomatoes, butter, cream, onion, garlic, ginger, and a blend of aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and red chilli powder. Dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) are a signature finishing ingredient that impart a distinctive slightly bitter, herbaceous note. The sauce is characteristically smooth, deep orange-red in colour, mildly spiced, and luxuriously creamy. Paneer Makhani is a vegetarian and gluten-free dish, widely popular across India, Singapore, and the global Indian diaspora. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 217 kcal, 8.3g protein, 16.7g fat (of which 9.3g is saturated fat from butter and cream), and 8.3g carbohydrate with a low glycemic index of approximately 45. Calcium content is notable at 117mg/100g from paneer. The high saturated fat content makes this dish a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease; patients should request reduced butter and cream. For Type 2 Diabetes, the low carbohydrate and low GI profile are favourable when portion size is controlled. Commonly served with basmati rice, naan, roti, or paratha.

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood. Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN paneer and curry ingredient data and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central for butter and cream components. GI is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables as no direct Paneer Makhani GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney database. Cultural metadata in the input (Turkish cuisine, Turkish ethnic tags, Singapore country of origin) were incorrect and have been corrected to Indian cuisine, North India/Punjab origin.
notes: 1. Input cultural metadata was significantly incorrect: primary_cuisine was listed as 'Turkish', ethnic_tags included 'Turkish', 'Kebab', 'Shawarma', and country_of_origin was 'Singapore'. All corrected to Indian cuisine, North India/Punjab region of origin. 2. Two duplicate portions were listed in the input (both labelled '1 bowl', 300g and 350g, both is_default=true); the 300g portion is adopted as the standard serving size as the more conservative estimate. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Paneer Makhani composite is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish entry; individual ingredient fatty acid data would require a full recipe-level calculation not supported by available approved sources. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values for the prepared Paneer Makhani composite are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single prepared-dish entry as of 2026. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~45, low) based on Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct GI measurement for Paneer Makhani exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 used as broad anchor (curry dish, closest_parent match); FoodEx2 A0CKP used as closest_parent anchor (cheese-based dish) — no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for Paneer Makhani. No SNOMED CT code available. 6. All core macros (protein_g 8.33, fat_g 16.67, carbohydrate_g 8.33) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a rich paneer curry; no zero-macro justification required.
